perception.who_is_this_for_a: A Disney trip planner who wants a simple alert service, with later comparison details for Pro users.
perception.who_is_this_for_b: A Disney dining planner who wants to start an alert immediately, with visible paths for families, superfans, and agents.

reasoning: Candidate B better satisfies the first-viewport obligation. It shows the product category, no-login reassurance, primary free alert action, and an actual alert setup form immediately on desktop. It also makes the sample alert concrete and reinforces the official Disney handoff. Candidate A is cleaner and has strong full-page pricing/comparison structure, but its first viewport is more passive: the CTA exists, yet the user cannot begin entering restaurant, dates, or party size until later, and the trust proof is mostly text chips rather than a task path. On mobile, B preserves a prominent no-login statement and large CTA before the sample alert, while A also does this reasonably but feels more like a static marketing page. B’s full page also supports segment self-selection more explicitly with planner-type cards, restaurant coverage, safety FAQ, Pro comparison, plans, testimonials, and an agency form. B has some visual issues, especially a repeated CTA/button visible near the desktop fold and a somewhat dense long page, but strategically it aligns more strongly with the diagnosis.
blocking_visual_issue_a: The first viewport does not expose the actual alert setup inputs, so the primary action is less immediate than required.
blocking_visual_issue_b: Desktop first viewport shows a duplicated large Start Free Alert button at the bottom edge, creating visual clutter and possible hierarchy confusion.
